title: Special Dunder Methods
subtitle: Make your objects behave like built-in Python types
learning_objectives: Understand what dunder (double underscore) methods are
Implement __str__ for a human-readable string representation
Implement __len__ so len() works on your object
Implement __eq__ so == compares two objects meaningfully

sections.heading: What Are Dunder Methods?
sections.content: Dunder methods (short for 'double underscore') are special methods with names like __str__, __len__, __eq__. They let your objects work with Python's built-in operators and functions. For example, implementing __str__ controls what print(your_object) shows.

sections.heading: __str__ and __repr__
sections.content: __str__ controls what you see when you print() an object — it should be human-friendly. __repr__ is for developers and debugging — it should show how to recreate the object.

sections.heading: __len__ and __eq__
sections.content: __len__ lets len() work on your object. __eq__ controls what == does.

exercises.title: Add __str__ to a Class
exercises.prompt: Add a __str__ method to the Book class so print(book) shows: 'Python Basics by Alice (250 pages)'. Create book = Book('Python Basics', 'Alice', 250) and print it.
hints: Use an f-string: f"{self.title} by {self.author} ({self.pages} pages)"
When you print(book), Python calls book.__str__() automatically

tests.message: Output: Python Basics by Alice (250 pages)

exercises.title: Implement __len__
exercises.prompt: Add a __len__ method to the ShoppingCart class that returns the number of items. Create cart = ShoppingCart(), add 3 items, then print len(cart).
hints: return len(self.items) — the length of the items list
Then len(cart) will automatically call your __len__ method

quiz.question: What does __str__ control?
options: How the object is stored in memory
How the object is compared with ==
How many items the object contains
quiz.explanation: __str__ returns a human-readable string representation of the object. When you call print(obj), Python automatically calls obj.__str__() and displays the result.

quiz.question: After implementing __len__, what built-in Python function will work on your object?
quiz.explanation: Implementing __len__ allows the built-in len() function to work on your objects. Python calls your_object.__len__() whenever you write len(your_object).

completion_message: Fantastic! Dunder methods make your classes feel like native Python — very professional!
